1: THE INTERVIEW — ask the player, in conversation, never as a form. Draw out: a NAME to carry as handle — lowercase, one word; read passport:<name> at this world first, and if it exists the name is taken, choose another. What they can DO — read the world's rules block (rules:*) and calibrate capability in ITS terms (the reference system scores Character Force in bands; a new arrival sits near the world's ordinary, with one peak and one weak spot — ask for both). What they WANT — the drive that outlives a session; one honest sentence. How they LOOK to a stranger — the appearance others will perceive before any name is earned. What they CARRY of a past — at most two or three names or places known before arriving (the knows seed; keep it thin — knowledge is earned in play, not declared). Then read the world's spatial block (spatial:<world>) and offer the arrival: the places whose prose RECEIVES strangers — a road ends at an inn, a gate admits, a crossing gathers. The player chooses where the road delivers them.
2: THE WRITES — the character becomes blocks at this world's beach, each created LOCKED under a passphrase the player chooses (creation with new_lock locks at birth — the passphrase is their authority over this character ever after; never echo it back into conversation once set). passport:<handle> — 1: capability in the world's own terms; 2: what they want; 3: the appearance sentence, then the location in the exact form ' Location: *:<beach-url>:spatial:<world>:<digit-path>' — the digit-path is the PLAIN run of digits walked from the spatial root to the place, one digit per level (a common room reached by 1, then 1, then 1 is written 111): never dotted, never comma'd — a dot in a location strands the character at the wrong scale — and COPIED from the spatial block, never guessed. knows:<handle> — {1: people they can name, 2: places they know} — the opening seed only. purpose:<handle> — the drive, one line at the underscore. witnessed:<handle> — their ARRIVAL MEMORY as the first entry, appended (append=true creates the account with its floor): how the road brought them here, in their own private voice. Write nothing else; the rest of a character is earned.
3: THE ARRIVAL — the character enters exactly as a mover completes a move, with no room left behind. Re-enter: pscale_play(world, handle) — the envelope now knows the character, hands the room at the arrival place, and shows who stands there. Commit an ARRIVING-BEAT to that room's pool: the public fact of a stranger entering, rendered by APPEARANCE — the name is not public until spoken aloud in the fiction. From that beat the operating directive carries the turn: perceive, act, commit. The character is no longer new; they are present, and the world has one more living hand.
4: ARRIVING TOGETHER — a party is a shared arrival place, nothing more. Each player walks this passage; all choose the same place; order does not matter — the early meet the room first (the standing figures, whoever else is present) and the late catch up from the public record. Co-presence at a shared address IS the introduction: no ceremony, no lobby, the room does the work. A party that wants a private start asks an author for a place of their own first (a commissioned region with its own hooks) and arrives there.
9: THE GATE — this door stands open by default: any player may walk it at any open world, and creation is admission enough. A world MAY gate it — a payway ticket at the world's collective buying the walk (face-bound, per the payway convention) — and the conventions for allowing and disallowing creation and authoring land WITH the payway implementation, not before. Until a world declares its gate, the passage is free and the lock a character is born under is the only authority that matters.
_: GENESIS — the door where a person becomes a character. Walked ONCE per handle per world: the passage from no-blocks to a living, locked, placed character. A world is JOINED, never booted — a character arrives INTO an ongoing world, at a place whose nature receives strangers; the world does not restart for anyone. Creation is rare and consequential — a hatching, not a signup: everything after this door grows by play, and the character's whereabouts are thereafter tracked by one address (passport position 3). Movements: the interview (1), the writes (2), the arrival (3); a party arriving together (4); what gates the door (9). Run the interview WITH the player — their answers, their character; you hold the pen, they hold the person.
